responses.strategic_rationale: The plan’s core tensions balance scientific ambition with participant safety, data quality, and operational sustainability. The five levers address: (1) scalable yet safe residential operations; (2) multi-modal sensing to capture sporadic parasomnia events while managing intrusiveness; (3) efficient event triage to reduce manual review without sacrificing accuracy; (4) ethical, diverse, and sustained recruitment with retention controls; (5) governance, funding diversification, and responsible partnerships. Together, they equalize quality, cost, and speed, enabling longitudinal phenotyping critical for aims while preserving privacy and compliance. The levers progress from conservative to radical, embedding checks for data governance, staff workload, and capital constraints to avoid over-commitment.

levers.name: Residential-Unit Operations Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Safety incidents drop with protocols; Systemic: capacity utilization rises 15% and false-alarm rate falls 20% due to standardized workflows; Strategic: enables scalable expansion but increases capex and renovation times.
options: Maintain eight suites with enhanced safety, fixed staffing, and standard data workflows, prioritizing reliability over expansion.
Expand to ten to twelve suites via phased renovations, broaden staffing coverage, and upgrade data governance while maintaining current privacy constraints.

Implement modular living-lab pods with on-site edge data processing, autonomous safety overrides, and a data marketplace licensing external researchers, enabling scalable, privacy-preserving full-scale operations.
levers.review_lever: Controls Safety & Comfort vs. Expansion & Data Yield. Weakness: The options fail to consider supply chain resilience and capital planning risks during rapid pod-based expansion.

levers.name: Sensing & Data Acquisition Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Rich data streams reveal episodes; Systemic: data quality improves by 25% and positives drop 15% due to multi-modal fusion; Strategic: accelerates phenotyping and tool development but raises governance burdens.
options: Maintain low-burden monitoring using dry-electrode headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, and discreet infrared cameras, ensuring high comfort and privacy while accepting limited physiological resolution.
Implement scheduled enhanced nights with full PSG on every participant at three time points while preserving flexibility to cancel if discomfort arises.

Deploy integrated, privacy-preserving on-site sensor fusion with edge processing, a data-sharing marketplace for approved researchers, and dynamic consent management to maximize data quality and external collaboration.
levers.review_lever: Controls Data Richness vs Participant Burden & Privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant fatigue and consent complexity for multi-modal sensing.

levers.name: Event-Triage & Scoring Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Triage throughput increases, reducing reviewer backlog; Systemic: adjudicated event yield improves by 40% with consistent criteria; Strategic: accelerates publication-ready datasets but raises risk of over-reliance on automated judgments.
options: Maintain dual-scored, human-driven event adjudication with structured criteria, avoiding automated triage to guarantee reliability and minimize misclassification.
Introduce rule-based semi-automation for triage with periodic expert audits, reducing reviewer workload while preserving accuracy through cross-validated thresholds and clear escalation protocols.

Deploy on-site automated feature extraction with cloud-backed adaptive scoring and continuous external benchmarking; ensure clinician-in-the-loop with rapid review; monetize performance gains via shared research credits.
levers.review_lever: Controls Throughput vs Diagnostic Accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential bias in automated triage and need for periodic external audits.

levers.name: Recruitment, Consent & Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enrollment reach improves as channels broaden; Systemic: retention rates rise 25% and completion rates improve due to flexible support; Strategic: accelerates data collection but increases complexity and regulatory exposure.
options: Strict pre-admission screening with written consent, hospital-based recruitment, and fixed per-night compensation coupled with predictable visit scheduling to maximize reliability and retention.
Expand recruitment to regional clinics and patient advocacy groups, offer flexible scheduling and enhanced participant support, plus tiered compensation tied to stay milestones to improve retention.

Establish participatory governance with participant-driven protocol options, sponsor-funded fellowships for international entrants, and a community-aligned, outcome-sharing funding model to boost enrollment and engagement.
levers.review_lever: Controls Diversity & Representativeness vs Safety & Compliance. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential inequity in access and support for non-local participants.

levers.name: Governance, Funding & Partnerships Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Funding diversification stabilizes cash flow; Systemic: co-funding exposes project to broader oversight and faster recruitment of partners; Strategic: spurs long-term growth but requires robust governance and alignment across institutions.
options: Maintain current external advisory board; limit external partnerships to core funders; preserve autonomy with strict budget controls.
Develop diversified funding with phased industry collaboration, philanthropic outreach, and a formal risk-sharing agreement; establish transparent reporting and governance for expanded oversight.

Adopt a joint venture governance model with a multi-stakeholder research consortium and data-sharing license revenue, enabling external contributions, cross-institutional secondments, and on-site incubator-style monetization of methods.
levers.review_lever: Controls Autonomy vs Oversight & Collaboration. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential conflicts of interest management and long-term reliance on partner funding.

responses.summary: The levers are well-balanced and progressive, challenging assumptions about rapid scale and autonomous judgments while anchoring safety and governance. One critical missing dimension is dynamic consent with participant-controlled data sharing; Add 'Dynamic consent governance and external data access framework' to Sensing & Data Acquisition Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: The five levers extend the prior plan by embedding adaptability, privacy-centric data ecosystems, workforce resilience, proactive ethics, and diversified funding into the research unit. They challenge assumptions that expansion must be linear, data access should be centralized, and governance can be static. Each lever adds a distinct axis: spatial-adaptation, data governance with monetization potential, resilient scheduling, real-time risk management, and sustainability via stakeholder finance. The progression from conservative to radical options tests safety, privacy, cost, and agility, enabling scalable phenotyping while maintaining integrity and compliance. Together, these levers diversify risk, unlock collaboration models, and improve data yield without compromising participant welfare or regulatory standards.

levers.name: Residential-Environment Adaptation Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enhanced environmental adaptation reduces minor safety incidents; Systemic: occupancy utilization rises 18% due to comfort and smoother workflows; Strategic: enables scalable adaptation but increases capex and renovation timelines, trading safety for speed.
options: Maintain eight suites with enhanced safety features, fixed staffing, and standard privacy controls, prioritizing reliability and regulatory compliance over expansion.
Phase in ten suites with scaled staffing and upgraded environmental controls, expanding data governance while preserving privacy limits and a calm residential ambiance.
Deploy modular environment pods with on-site edge data processing, autonomous safety overrides, and a data-as-a-service licensing model to enable rapid scalable expansion.
levers.review_lever: Controls Safety & Comfort vs. Expansion & Capex. Weakness: The options fail to consider supply chain resilience and capital planning during rapid pod-based expansion.

levers.name: Privacy-Preserving Data Ecosystem Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Privacy controls tightened reduce participant withdrawal; Systemic: data completion rises 22% due to streamlined consent and clearer studies; Strategic: promotes collaboration but increases governance overhead and compliance costs.
options: Maintain current privacy controls with strict data access governance and de-identification pipelines, accepting slower data release in exchange for regulatory comfort.
Introduce dynamic consent management allowing participant-driven data sharing within a controlled ecosystem, enabling higher data utilization while maintaining baseline privacy protections.
Create privacy-preserving on-site data marketplace with edge analytics and data-as-a-service licensing to external researchers, leveraging participant consent to monetize method development.
levers.review_lever: Controls Privacy/Consent vs Data Utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider consent fatigue and regulatory disparities across partners.

levers.name: Workforce Resilience & Scheduling Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Flexible scheduling improves staff morale and coverage stability; Systemic: turnover falls 15% and shift reliability increases; Strategic: reduces burnout risk but raises compensation costs and scheduling management complexity.
options: Maintain current night-shift structure with fixed staffing and standard burnout prevention policies.
Adopt flexible staggered shifts, cross-training, and telemedicine support to reduce fatigue and improve retention.
Implement autonomous staff augmentation with on-site robotic assistants and a workforce-as-a-service platform to cover peak demand.
levers.review_lever: Controls Workforce Resilience vs Operational Complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider staffing shortages and cross-training cost.

levers.name: Ethical Oversight & Risk Mitigation Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Continuous risk checks reduce unanticipated ethical issues; Systemic: audit cycles cut incidents by 20% and reporting lag halves; Strategic: strengthens trust but increases compliance overhead and review cycles.
options: Maintain existing ethics approvals with scheduled annual reviews and frontline reporting.
Institute continuous risk assessment with quarterly independent audits and stakeholder engagement.
Launch ethics-as-a-service platform delivering on-demand external audits funded by a blended subscription and milestone-based payments.
levers.review_lever: Controls Governance/Compliance vs Agility/Innovation. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory burden across multiple jurisdictions.

levers.name: Sustainability & Value Capture Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: External partnerships unlock pilot funding; Systemic: funding resilience improves by 25% and cost-sharing reduces single-source dependency; Strategic: accelerates long-term impact but introduces governance frictions and partner alignment challenges.
options: Pursue cost containment and in-kind contributions while maintaining self-contained operations.
Pursue revenue from data products by licensing de-identified datasets to vetted researchers under privacy constraints.
Adopt a data-sharing consortium with value-based licensing and a social impact financing model, funded through outcome-based investments and a coalition of funders to sustain long-term operations.
levers.review_lever: Controls Sustainability vs Funding fragility. Weakness: The options rely on external partners, risking continuity if partners withdraw.

responses.summary: The new levers add environmental adaptability, data-ecosystem monetization, workforce resilience, proactive ethics, and diversified funding to the research plan. One critical missing dimension remains dynamic consent governance across data streams; Add 'Dynamic consent governance and external data access framework' to Privacy-Preserving Data Ecosystem Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: The five new levers extend the plan by embedding participant-driven data governance, adaptive operations, external validation, clinician-facing interfaces, and open-science collaboration. They push toward flexible consent, scalable staffing, cross-site credibility, actionable visualization, and broader impact while maintaining safety, privacy, and compliance. The progression from conservative to radical options tests resilience against regulatory shifts, workforce pressures, and funding volatility, and promotes rigorous reproducibility alongside responsible data stewardship.

levers.name: Dynamic Consent Governance Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: participant autonomy expands via dynamic consent toggles; Systemic: consent operations require automation, increasing overhead by 12%; Strategic: data use agility improves, enabling faster collaborations but raising regulatory and audit demands.
options: Maintain fixed consent templates with annual renewals, minimal automation, and restricted external data access to uphold compliance.
Adopt dynamic consent scaffolding with tiered permissions, automated expiry, and consent-modulated data sharing across partner research groups.
Launch a dynamic, interoperable consent platform featuring consent-tokens, modular governance, and participant revenue-sharing to monetize consent while enabling cross-institution data exchange.
levers.review_lever: Controls Participant Autonomy vs Operational Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-institution interoperability and consent fatigue.

levers.name: Adaptive Staffing & Space Utilization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: staffing efficiency improves through cross-coverage; Systemic: occupancy utilization increases 15% and peak-load management stabilizes; Strategic: enables flexible growth but raises scheduling complexity, training demands, and potential staff burnout risk.
options: Maintain current staffing with fixed shifts and standard privacy controls, prioritizing reliability and minimal disruption over expansion.
Expand to ten suites with phased staffing, smart room allocation, and scalable shift patterns; maintain privacy controls while extending data governance and participant support.
Deploy autonomous scheduling powered by predictive analytics and modular space configurations, plus a revenue-sharing arrangement with university housing to fund rapid expansion.
levers.review_lever: Controls Staffing Flexibility vs Cost and Scheduling Complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term capital depreciation and burnout risk under high-flex schedules.

levers.name: External Validation & Replication Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: independent verification reduces bias; Systemic: replication across sites improves generalizability by 18% and confidence intervals narrow; Strategic: strengthens credibility but increases coordination overhead and potential delays in publication.
options: Establish formal data-sharing consortium with standardized annotation schemas, shared protocols, and joint data stewardship agreements across partners to enable rapid benchmarking.
Integrate cross-site replication with standardized hardware-gateway pods and a central data-ops platform offering automated audit trails and monetized access licenses for external researchers.
Enable cross-site replication via standardized data interfaces and a shared reproducibility dashboard, plus a subscription-based replication-as-a-service model for industry partners.
levers.review_lever: Controls Verification & Replication Pace vs Coordination Load. Weakness: The options risk IP ownership conflicts and onboarding delays across partners.

levers.name: Clinical-Data Interface & Visualization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: clinicians and researchers access unified data views; Systemic: reduced time-to-insight by 25% due to integrated dashboards; Strategic: improves decision support and collaboration but requires robust UX design and governance.
options: Maintain archival data access with static dashboards and standard export formats, ensuring reliability and privacy.
Develop interactive, role-based visualization layers with real-time annotation of events, enabling clinicians to review patterns quickly while enforcing access controls.

Launch an immersive visualization platform using augmented reality dashboards and on-device edge analytics, enabling clinicians to inspect events in-situ while monetizing via licensing to partner hospitals.
levers.review_lever: Controls Accessibility vs Security & Compliance. Weakness: The options may overfit to clinicians, risking data overload or inconsistent annotations.

levers.name: Open-Science Collaboration & Data Stewardship Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: external collaboration accelerates method development; Systemic: data stewardship improves through shared standards and governance; Strategic: broad impact increases but raises licensing, attribution complexity, and potential misalignment of partner priorities.
options: Restrict collaborations to core researchers with formal NDAs and fixed publication rights to maintain control and speed.
Establish a phased collaboration framework with transparent data-use agreements, shared credit models, and open-method publications to maximize impact and trust.
Create an on-site research incubator with revenue-sharing data products and cross-institution licenses, enabling rapid iteration while ensuring compliance and clear publication sequencing.
levers.review_lever: Controls Collaboration Reach vs Data Stewardship Risk. Weakness: The options may overlook alignment of partner priorities and attribution across institutions.

responses.summary: Missing dimension: explicit interoperability standards for cross-site data exchange and uniform event-annotation schemas across multi-modal streams. Concrete addition: Add 'Interoperability Protocol for cross-site data exchange and standardized event-annotation schema' to External Validation & Replication Strategy.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts that are specific to this project's domain (e.g., "[Domain]-[Decision Type] Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
